headline: How Citizen Scientists Solved Evolution's Parenting Mystery
meta_description: Volunteers on iNaturalist doubled research on parental care in harvestmen, revealing how motherhood and fatherhood evolved differently.
summary_short: Thousands of citizen observers helped researchers understand how parental care evolved in harvestmen—in just one week of searching.
body:
A team led by a University of São Paulo scientist needed answers about how parental care evolved in harvestmen, those spider-like arachnids found worldwide. But traditional research would have meant years of museum visits and fieldwork. Instead, they turned to iNaturalist, a platform where nature enthusiasts upload photographs and observations from virtually anywhere on Earth.

The results were startling. From 1936 through 2025, published studies had documented parental guarding in only 80 harvestman species. Using iNaturalist's public database, researchers more than doubled that number in just two days of searching—adding 62 new records contributed by citizen scientists alone.

The expanded dataset revealed something unexpected: maternal and paternal care followed entirely different evolutionary paths. Mothering evolved only from species with no parental care, mirroring patterns in insects. Fatherhood, however, could evolve either directly from non-caring ancestors or from species where females already guarded eggs—suggesting different pressures shaped each behavior.

Lead researcher Glauco Machado emphasized what this means for science globally: eliminating costly museum visits and extensive fieldwork makes large-scale biological research far more accessible, particularly for scientists in the Global South. Yet the team stresses that expert taxonomists remain irreplaceable—correctly identifying species and distinguishing true parental care from similar behaviors still requires specialized knowledge.

Citizen science isn't replacing expertise. It's accelerating discovery.
